serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of green milk tea (less sugar, no topping) is approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, providing about 103 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Green Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for green milk tea (less sugar, no topping), cr_id D08010036. All per-100g values preserved from the HPB canonical record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Milk Tea and Tea-Based Beverages with Sugar — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for green milk tea (less sugar, no topping). GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea beverages with moderate sugar content. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 or below is classified as LOW (0–55), GI 56–69 is medium, and GI 70+ is high; therefore gi_category is set to 'low'. The reduced sugar formulation (4.86 g sugar/100 g) and presence of milk fat and protein are expected to moderate the glycemic response relative to full-sugar variants. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 12.15) / 100 ≈ 6.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar with no dietary fibre in a sweetened beverage
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to the presence of added sugar (4.86 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre; the 'less sugar' designation does not exempt it from the sweetened beverage restriction, and a truly unsweetened green tea with low-fat milk is the recommended alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (4.86 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage
type2_diabetes.guidance: Per ADA guidelines, any beverage containing added sugar is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes regardless of the reduced sugar level; patients should request a truly unsweetened version or substitute with plain green tea and unsweetened low-fat milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar and fat content (2.41 g/100 g) with no fibre in a regularly consumed beverage
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sweetened milk tea beverages, even at reduced sugar levels, contributes to excess caloric and sugar intake that is associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate sweetened beverage consumption and opt for unsweetened tea alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with no anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This formulation contains added sugar (4.86 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre and no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened green tea with low-fat dairy milk, which would provide catechins and calcium beneficial for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Milk Tea (Less Sugar, No Topping) is a freshly prepared Singaporean speciality tea beverage made by combining brewed green tea with milk or non-dairy creamer and a reduced amount of added sugar, served without any customisable toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. It is a popular drink available at bubble tea shops and hawker stalls across Singapore. The beverage has a mild, slightly grassy green tea flavour balanced by the creaminess of milk or creamer, with a lighter sweetness compared to standard milk tea formulations. Per 100 g, it provides 41 kcal, 0.32 g protein, 2.41 g fat, 4.86 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 12 mg sodium. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, the beverage still contains added sugar at 4.86 g/100 g with no dietary fibre, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under clinical dietary guidelines. It is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to the presence of added sugar without fibre mitigation. The green tea component provides trace catechins and antioxidants, but these are insufficient to offset the metabolic impact of the added sugar in this formulation. A truly unsweetened version with low-fat milk would be a clinically preferable alternative for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact food item (cr_id D08010036, confidence 0.95). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are preserved from the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct GI entry exists for this specific variant.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source data for this formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient profile, and moisture data for this freshly prepared green milk tea variant are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been removed as this beverage contains 4.86 g carbohydrate/100 g (12.15 g per 250 ml serving) which is inconsistent with strict ketogenic dietary requirements. GI estimated at 55 using the closest analogous University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk tea; per the University of Sydney classification scale GI ≤55 is LOW, therefore gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 12.15) / 100 ≈ 6.7.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Added missing aliases_by_language entries to flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 falls at the boundary of the low category (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale. Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification rationale. Performed full aliases consistency self-check to confirm all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
